sections.title: Informal Affirmative Commands (tú)
sections.explanation: To tell a friend or family member to do something, use the affirmative tú command. For regular verbs, the form is identical to the third-person singular present tense (él/ella form): hablar → habla, comer → come, escribir → escribe. However, eight common verbs have irregular affirmative tú commands that must be memorized: decir → di, hacer → haz, ir → ve, poner → pon, salir → sal, ser → sé, tener → ten, venir → ven. These eight irregulars are sometimes remembered with the mnemonic 'Di Haz Ve Pon Sal Sé Ten Ven'. Note that subject pronouns are usually omitted in commands.
sections.tip: A handy trick: for regular verbs, just drop 'tú' from the present tense sentence and you have the command. 'Tú hablas' → '¡Habla!' The eight irregulars (di, haz, ve, pon, sal, sé, ten, ven) are extremely common — learn them as a set.

examples.es: Pon el libro en la mesa.
examples.en: Put the book on the table.

exercises.prompt: Your friend is speaking too quietly. Which command tells them to speak louder?
exercises.explanation: The affirmative tú command of 'hablar' is 'habla' — the same as the él/ella present tense form. Never use the infinitive or the tú present form as a command.

exercises.prompt: ___ (hacer) tu cama antes de salir. (Make your bed before leaving.)
exercises.hint: 'hacer' is one of the eight irregular tú commands
exercises.explanation: hacer → haz (irregular). The eight irregular affirmative tú commands are: di, haz, ve, pon, sal, sé, ten, ven.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct affirmative tú command for 'comer' (to eat)?
exercises.explanation: 'comer' is regular, so its tú command equals the él/ella present form: com + e = come. 'coma' is the usted command form.

exercises.prompt: ___ (venir) a la fiesta esta noche. (Come to the party tonight.)
exercises.hint: 'venir' is one of the eight irregulars
exercises.explanation: venir → ven (irregular tú command). Do not confuse with 'viene' (he/she comes).

exercises.prompt: You want your sibling to put away their backpack. Which sentence is correct?
options: Poner tu mochila en su lugar.
Pon tu mochila en su lugar.
Pone tu mochila en su lugar.
exercises.explanation: poner → pon (irregular tú command). 'Pone' is the él/ella present indicative, not a command; the infinitive 'poner' is never used as a command on its own.

exercises.prompt: ___ (escribir) una carta a tu abuela. (Write a letter to your grandmother.)
exercises.hint: 'escribir' is regular — use the él/ella present form
exercises.explanation: escribir → escribe (regular tú command: escrib + e). Regular -IR verbs form tú commands just like their él/ella present tense.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is NOT one of the eight irregular affirmative tú commands?
exercises.explanation: 'habla' is a regular tú command (él/ella form of hablar). The eight irregulars are: di, haz, ve, pon, sal, sé, ten, ven.

exercises.prompt: ___ (salir) ahora o llegarás tarde. (Leave now or you'll be late.)
exercises.hint: 'salir' is one of the eight irregulars
exercises.explanation: salir → sal (irregular tú command). The infinitive 'salir' would not be grammatically correct as a command here.

sections.title: Informal Negative Commands (tú)
sections.explanation: To tell someone NOT to do something with tú, use 'no' plus the present subjunctive tú form. To form the present subjunctive for most verbs: start with the yo present indicative, drop the -o, then add opposite vowel endings — -AR verbs get -e endings (hablar → hablo → habl- → no hables), and -ER/-IR verbs get -a endings (comer → como → com- → no comas; escribir → escribo → escrib- → no escribas). Stem-changing verbs keep their stem change in the subjunctive. Irregular yo-form verbs (like tener → tengo, decir → digo) use that irregular stem: no tengas, no digas.
sections.tip: Remember: affirmative tú commands use the él/ella present form, but negative tú commands use the subjunctive. This is the key difference — 'Habla' (speak!) vs. 'No hables' (don't speak!). The 'yo-form trick' makes forming the subjunctive easy: drop the -o from yo and swap the vowel.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Don't eat meat' to a friend (tú)?
exercises.explanation: Negative tú commands use the present subjunctive: comer → yo como → com- + as = comas. 'No come' is wrong (that is the affirmative command). 'No comer' uses the infinitive, which is not correct here.

exercises.prompt: No ___ (hablar) con la boca llena. (Don't talk with your mouth full.)
exercises.hint: Use the present subjunctive tú form: hablar → yo hablo → habl- + es
exercises.explanation: hablar → yo hablo → drop -o → habl- + es = hables. -AR verbs take -e endings in the subjunctive.

exercises.prompt: Your little brother is about to run into traffic. Which command is correct?
exercises.explanation: correr → yo corro → corr- + as = corras. Negative tú command: no corras. The form 'corres' is the present indicative tú, not a command.

exercises.prompt: No ___ (escribir) en las paredes. (Don't write on the walls.)
exercises.hint: escribir → yo escribo → escrib- + as (opposite vowel for -IR verbs)
exercises.explanation: escribir → yo escribo → drop -o → escrib- + as = escribas. -IR verbs also take -a endings in the subjunctive.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'Don't go out tonight' to a friend?
exercises.explanation: salir → yo salgo → drop -o → salg- + as = salgas. Irregular yo forms carry over into the subjunctive stem. 'No sal' would be wrong because 'sal' is the affirmative command, not a negative one.

exercises.prompt: No ___ (tener) miedo del examen. (Don't be afraid of the exam.)
exercises.hint: tener → yo tengo → drop -o → teng- + as
exercises.explanation: tener → yo tengo → teng- + as = tengas. Because the yo form is irregular (tengo), the subjunctive stem is also irregular: teng-.

exercises.prompt: Compare: 'Habla más' vs. 'No hables más'. What is the difference?
options: 'Habla' uses the subjunctive; 'hables' uses the indicative.
Both mean the same thing but 'no hables' is more polite.
'Habla más' is an affirmative command (speak more); 'No hables más' is a negative command (don't speak more).
exercises.explanation: Affirmative tú commands use the él/ella indicative form (habla). Negative tú commands use 'no' + present subjunctive (no hables). The two forms are different and cannot be swapped.

exercises.prompt: No ___ (decir) eso nunca más. (Don't say that ever again.)
exercises.hint: decir → yo digo → drop -o → dig- + as
exercises.explanation: decir → yo digo → dig- + as = digas. Another example of an irregular yo form giving an irregular subjunctive stem.

sections.title: Formal Commands (usted / ustedes)
sections.explanation: When speaking formally (to strangers, elders, or in professional settings), use usted (singular) or ustedes (plural). Both formal command forms always use the present subjunctive: for usted, use the present subjunctive él/ella form; for ustedes, use the present subjunctive ellos/ellas form. Formation: take the yo present indicative, drop -o, and add opposite vowel endings — -AR verbs: usted -e, ustedes -en (hablar → hable, hablen); -ER/-IR verbs: usted -a, ustedes -an (comer → coma, coman; escribir → escriba, escriban). The same yo-form trick applies for irregulars: tener → tengo → usted tenga, ustedes tengan. To make them negative, simply add 'no': no hable, no coman.
sections.tip: Unlike tú commands (which have separate affirmative and negative forms), usted/ustedes commands always use the subjunctive — both positive and negative. There is no separate form to memorize. Think: formal = always subjunctive.

exercises.prompt: You are a doctor addressing a patient (usted). How do you say 'Take this medicine'?
exercises.explanation: tomar → yo tomo → tom- + e = tome (usted command). 'Toma' is the informal tú command. In formal contexts always use the subjunctive form.

exercises.prompt: ___ (hablar) con el gerente si tiene alguna queja. (Speak with the manager if you have a complaint.) [usted]
exercises.hint: hablar → yo hablo → habl- + e (usted subjunctive)
exercises.explanation: hablar → yo hablo → habl- + e = hable. This is the usted affirmative command using the present subjunctive.

exercises.prompt: A teacher tells the entire class (ustedes): 'Open your books.' Which is correct?
exercises.explanation: abrir → yo abro → abr- + an = abran (ustedes command). 'Abre' is the informal tú command. 'Abra' is the usted (singular) command.

exercises.prompt: No ___ (llegar) tarde a la reunión. (Don't arrive late to the meeting.) [usted]
exercises.hint: llegar → yo llego → llegu- + e. Note the spelling change g → gu before e
exercises.explanation: llegar → yo llego → stem llegu- (the g needs a u before e to preserve the hard g sound) + e = llegue. No llegue = don't arrive (usted).

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly tells a group of guests (ustedes) to 'sit down'?
exercises.explanation: sentarse (to sit down) → ustedes command: siéntense (reflexive ustedes command with -se attached). 'Siéntate' is the informal tú reflexive command. Infinitives are not used as commands.

exercises.prompt: ___ (escribir) sus respuestas en papel. (Write your answers on paper.) [ustedes]
exercises.hint: escribir → yo escribo → escrib- + an (ustedes, -IR verb gets -an)
exercises.explanation: escribir → yo escribo → escrib- + an = escriban. -IR verbs use -an for the ustedes command.

exercises.prompt: A hotel receptionist tells a guest (usted): 'Have a good night.' Which form is correct?
exercises.explanation: tener → yo tengo → teng- + a = tenga (usted command). 'Ten' is the irregular informal tú command. 'Tiene' is the present indicative, not a command.

exercises.prompt: No ___ (hablar) entre ustedes durante el examen. (Don't talk among yourselves during the exam.) [ustedes]
exercises.hint: hablar → habl- + en (ustedes subjunctive, -AR verb gets -en)
exercises.explanation: hablar → yo hablo → habl- + en = hablen. To make a formal command negative, simply add 'no': no hablen.
